```{r}
  n <- length(labels)
  tab <- table(labels)
  tiny <- as.integer(names(tab)[tab < n * min_pct])
  if (length(tiny) == 0) return(labels)
  big <- as.integer(names(tab)[tab >= n * min_pct])
  if (length(big) == 0) return(labels)
  cen <- sapply(big, function(i) colMeans(sc[labels == i, , drop = FALSE]))
  for (ti in tiny) {
    pts <- which(labels == ti)
    cent_ti <- colMeans(sc[pts, , drop = FALSE])
    nearest <- big[which.min(colSums((cen - cent_ti)^2))]
    labels[pts] <- nearest
  }
  as.integer(factor(labels, levels = sort(unique(labels))))
}

raw_feat <- cluster_pre$raw_features
sc_merge <- scale(as.matrix(raw_feat[, pc$features]))
merged_labels <- merge_tiny_clusters(raw_labels_k5, sc_merge)

cat("After merge:\n")
print(table(merged_labels))

# Visualise comparison
d_before <- data.frame(PC1 = pca_df$PC1, PC2 = pca_df$PC2,
                       cluster = factor(raw_labels_k5), stage = "Before Merge (k=5)")
d_after <- data.frame(PC1 = pca_df$PC1, PC2 = pca_df$PC2,
                      cluster = factor(merged_labels), stage = "After Merge")
d_compare <- rbind(d_before, d_after)
keep_c <- trim_pct(d_compare$PC1) & trim_pct(d_compare$PC2)
d_compare <- d_compare[keep_c, ]

set.seed(42)
d_compare <- do.call(rbind, lapply(split(d_compare, d_compare$stage), function(df) {
  if (nrow(df) > 4000) df[sample(nrow(df), 4000), ] else df
}))

ggplot(d_compare, aes(x = PC1, y = PC2, color = cluster)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.3, size = 0.8) +
  scale_color_manual(values = cluster_pal) +
  facet_wrap(~stage) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(strip.text = element_text(face = "bold", size = 11),
        legend.position = "bottom")
```

**Why merge?** Micro-clusters (20-30 members out of 48K) have no business value — sample sizes are too small for robust profiling, and they appear as nearly invisible dots on PCA plots. The merge assigns tiny cluster members to the nearest large cluster by centroid distance.

## Algorithm Comparison (3-panel)

```{r}
#| label: fig-algo-compare
#| fig-cap: "PCA scatter by algorithm (RFM, k=4) — assessing segment robustness across methods"
#| fig-height: 4

algo_list <- c("kmeans", "hclust", "pam")
algo_names <- c("K-Means", "Hierarchical (Ward)", "PAM (CLARA)")

algo_dfs <- lapply(seq_along(algo_list), function(i) {
  lab <- pc$methods[[algo_list[i]]]$labels[, "k4"]
  data.frame(PC1 = pca_df$PC1, PC2 = pca_df$PC2,
             cluster = factor(lab), algorithm = algo_names[i])
})
algo_df <- do.call(rbind, algo_dfs)
keep_a <- trim_pct(algo_df$PC1) & trim_pct(algo_df$PC2)
algo_df <- algo_df[keep_a, ]
set.seed(42)
algo_df <- do.call(rbind, lapply(split(algo_df, algo_df$algorithm), function(df) {
  if (nrow(df) > 4000) df[sample(nrow(df), 4000), ] else df
}))

ggplot(algo_df, aes(x = PC1, y = PC2, color = cluster)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.3, size = 0.8) +
  scale_color_manual(values = cluster_pal) +
  facet_wrap(~algorithm, ncol = 3) +
  labs(color = "Cluster") +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(legend.position = "bottom",
        strip.text = element_text(face = "bold", size = 11))
```

When all three algorithms produce **similar cluster boundaries**, the segments reflect genuine data structure rather than algorithmic artifacts.

## Income Distribution per Cluster

```{r}
#| label: fig-income-dist
#| fig-cap: "Income Bracket Composition within Each Cluster"

demo_df <- data.frame(cluster = factor(labels_k4),
                      income_bracket = demo$income_bracket)
d_inc <- demo_df %>%
  count(cluster, income_bracket) %>%
  group_by(cluster) %>%
  mutate(pct = n / sum(n))

p_inc <- ggplot(d_inc, aes(
  x = cluster, y = pct, fill = income_bracket,
  text = paste0("Cluster ", cluster, " | ", income_bracket,
                ": ", round(pct*100, 1), "% (n=", n, ")"))) +
  geom_col(position = "fill", alpha = 0.85, width = 0.6) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = income_colors) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = label_percent()) +
  labs(x = "Cluster", y = NULL, fill = "Income Bracket") +
  theme_minimal() + theme(legend.position = "bottom")

ggplotly(p_inc, tooltip = "text")
```

## Centroids Table

```{r}
#| label: tbl-centroids
feat_df2 <- raw_feat %>% select(all_of(pc$features))
feat_df2$cluster <- factor(labels_k4)
cm2 <- feat_df2 %>%
  group_by(cluster) %>%
  summarise(across(everything(), mean), count = n(), .groups = "drop") %>%
  rename(Cluster = cluster, Count = count)

num_cols <- names(cm2)[sapply(cm2, is.numeric) & names(cm2) != "Count"]

datatable(cm2,
          options = list(pageLength = 10, scrollX = TRUE, dom = "t"),
          rownames = FALSE, class = "compact stripe hover",
          caption = "Cluster Centroids: Mean Feature Values and Size") %>%
  formatRound(columns = num_cols, digits = 2) %>%
  formatStyle("Count", fontWeight = "bold", color = "#f97316")
```

# UI Design: Parameters, Outputs & Component Selection

## Exposed Parameters and Outputs

### Parameters Exposed to Users

| Parameter | Why Exposed | Default | Range |
|-----------|------------|---------|-------|
| Feature Preset | Different presets answer different business questions | RFM | rfm / behavioral / engagement / custom |
| Number of Clusters (k) | Core analytical choice | 4 | 2–8 |
| Algorithm | Different algorithms suit different data shapes | K-Means | K-Means / Hierarchical / PAM |
| Color By | Overlay demographics to validate cluster meaning | Cluster | Cluster / Income / Segment / CLV / Churn Risk |
| Custom Features | Advanced users may want non-preset combinations | 5 of 10 | 10 checkboxes |

### Outputs Exposed

| Output | Type | Purpose |
|--------|------|---------|
| PCA Scatter | `plotlyOutput` | Primary cluster visualisation |
| Elbow Plot | `plotOutput` | WSS diagnostic for k selection |
| Silhouette Plot | `plotOutput` | Cluster quality validation |
| Feature Profiles | `plotlyOutput` | Cross-cluster feature comparison |
| Income Distribution | `plotlyOutput` | Demographic composition check |
| Size Warning | `uiOutput` | Alert when cluster < 2% |
| Centroids Table | `DTOutput` | Precise numeric detail |
| Export CSV | `downloadButton` | Data export for downstream use |

## Shiny Component Selection

### Sidebar Controls

| UI Element | Shiny Widget | Why This Widget |
|------------|-------------|----------------|
| Feature Preset | `selectInput` | 4 mutually exclusive choices — dropdown is more compact than radio buttons |
| Number of Clusters | `sliderInput` | Continuous range 2–8, slider provides immediate visual feedback of position |
| Algorithm | `radioButtons` | 3 choices always visible — radio buttons better than dropdown when ≤5 options |
| Color By | `selectInput` | 5 choices — dropdown saves vertical space in the sidebar |
| Custom Features | `checkboxGroupInput` | Multiple non-exclusive selections — checkboxes are the standard pattern |
| Select All / Clear | `actionButton` pair | Batch toggles for checkbox group — prevents tedious individual clicking |
| Run Clustering | `actionButton(class="btn-primary")` | Primary CTA, only visible in custom mode via `conditionalPanel` |

### Layout Structure

```{mermaid}
%%| fig-width: 8
flowchart TD
    A["layout_sidebar (sidebar = 270px)"] --> B["layout_columns (8 : 4)"]
    A --> C["layout_columns (6 : 6)"]
    A --> D["card (full-width)"]

    B --> B1["card: PCA Cluster Visualization\n plotlyOutput · 380px"]
    B --> B2["card: Cluster Quality Diagnostics\n navset_tab"]
    B2 --> B2a["Elbow · plotOutput · 160px"]
    B2 --> B2b["Silhouette · plotOutput · 160px"]

    C --> C1["card: Feature Profiles\n plotlyOutput · 340px"]
    C --> C2["card: Income Distribution\n plotlyOutput · 340px"]

    D --> D1["Centroids Summary + Export CSV\n DTOutput + downloadButton"]

    style A fill:#f97316,color:#fff,stroke:none
    style B fill:#a855f7,color:#fff,stroke:none
    style C fill:#a855f7,color:#fff,stroke:none
    style D fill:#a855f7,color:#fff,stroke:none
```

### Reactive Architecture

```{mermaid}
%%| fig-width: 9
flowchart LR
    P["cluster_preset"] --> PRE
    K["cluster_k"] --> PRE
    M["cluster_method"] --> PRE

    PRE["cl_precomputed() \n instant lookup"] --> RES

    V["cluster_vars"] --> CUS
    G["cluster_go"] --> CUS

    CUS["cl_custom() \n live computation"] --> RES

    RES["cl_result()"] --> O1["cl_pca"] & O2["cl_elbow"] & O3["cl_silhouette"] & O4["cl_profile"] & O5["cl_demo"] & O6["cl_table"]

    CLR["cluster_color"] --> O1

    style PRE fill:#a855f7,color:#fff,stroke:#7c3aed,stroke-width:2px
    style CUS fill:#fb7185,color:#fff,stroke:#e11d48,stroke-width:2px
    style RES fill:#f97316,color:#fff,stroke:#ea580c,stroke-width:2px
```

**Pre-computation strategy**: 3 presets × 3 algorithms × 7 k-values = **63 combinations** computed offline in `data_prep.R`. The `.rds` file loads in <1 second. Only the "Custom" preset triggers live computation.

## Storyboard Wireframes

### Customer Segmentation Module

![](images/segmentation_wireframe.png){fig-align="center" width="95%"}

The wireframe shows a **sidebar + four-quadrant main panel** layout: PCA scatter (top-left), quality diagnostics (top-right), feature profiles (bottom-left), demographics (bottom-right), with a full-width centroids table below.

### Design System Reference

![](images/design_system.png){fig-align="center" width="85%"}

The design system defines the visual language shared across all modules: a dark-themed palette (`#0f172a` background, warm accent colors), typography hierarchy, card styling with `bslib::card()`, and consistent color mapping for clusters and demographic variables.

### About Page

![](images/about.png){fig-align="center" width="85%"}

The About page introduces the COFINFAD project background, team member responsibilities, data sources, and methodology overview. I designed it as a single scrollable page with section cards, providing context for users unfamiliar with the application.

# References

- Fader, P. S., Hardie, B. G. S., & Lee, K. L. (2005). RFM and CLV: Using Iso-value Curves for Customer Base Analysis. *Journal of Marketing Research*, 42(4), 415–430.
- Few, S. (2012). Radar Graphs: Avoid Them (99.9% of the Time). *Perceptual Edge*, Visual Business Intelligence Newsletter.
- Munzner, T. (2014). *Visualization Analysis and Design*. CRC Press.
- Wedel, M., & Kamakura, W. A. (2000). *Market Segmentation: Conceptual and Methodological Foundations* (2nd ed.). Kluwer Academic Publishers.
- Wickham, H. (2013). Overplotting. In *ggplot2: Elegant Graphics for Data Analysis*. Springer.
